content: {"answer": "The search results document multiple population-specific safety concerns for IF. For pregnancy, Source 1 reports IF (including Ramadan fasting) during pregnancy was associated with a 'minor but statistically significant' reduction in neonatal birth weight; Source 8 notes birth weight and preterm risk were not significantly affected but placental weight was significantly lower with fasting; Sources 6 and 10 list IF contraindications in pregnancy (nutritional deficiency risk, possible preterm birth, low blood sugar, increased maternal energy needs); Source 9 states IF 'is generally not recommended to women during pregnancy' with limited research. For lactation, Source 4 (preclinical) found CR and IF during lactation led to marked undernutrition in offspring persistent through weaning into adolescence; Sources 2, 5, 7 (lay medical outlets) warn IF can reduce milk supply and is potentially dangerous during breastfeeding; Source 3 adds additional caution for mothers with diabetes, other health conditions, or strenuous jobs. For sulfonylurea users, Source 11 reports a case of severe relapsing sulphonylurea-induced hypoglycemia in a 64-year-old woman; Source 13 documents prolonged hypoglycemia with sulfonylurea combined with certain antibiotics \u2014 both highlight the hypoglycemia risk class but do not specifically test sulfonylurea-plus-IF. For T2DM, Source 14 found 9-hour TRE appeared safe for up to 5 days/week in a small group; Source 15 (Chadwick 2025) included fasting-blood-glucose monitoring protocol for IF arms in T2DM. Source 12 is a registered TRE-in-T2DM trial. The sources do NOT directly address adolescents, shift workers as a distinct group, or IF in eating-disorder history, beyond general cautions. On communication: no source provides specific primary-care counseling guidance. Overall the evidence supports clear contraindications in pregnancy, lactation, and insulin/sulfonylurea users, and provides cautious short-term safety data in T2DM under monitored TRE; evidence gaps persist for adolescents, shift workers, and eating-disorder history.", "key_findings": ["IF in pregnancy (including Ramadan fasting): minor but statistically significant reduction in neonatal birth weight; significantly lower placental weight reported (Sources 1, 8); general non-recommendation guidance (Sources 6, 9, 10).", "Preclinical: CR and IF during lactation caused marked undernutrition in offspring persistent through weaning into adolescence (Source 4); lay medical sources warn milk-supply reduction (Sources 2, 5, 7).", "Sulfonylurea users: documented severe relapsing sulphonylurea-induced hypoglycemia cases (Sources 11, 13) \u2014 drug class at intrinsic hypoglycemia risk that IF could aggravate.", "T2DM: 9-hour TRE appears safe for up to 5 days/week in a small study with glucose monitoring (Source 14); active trials ongoing (Sources 12, 15).", "Evidence gaps: no direct sources surfaced for adolescents, shift workers as a distinct cohort, or IF in eating-disorder history beyond general cautions."], "sources_used": ["https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12655342/", "https://lilynicholsrdn.com/intermittent-fasting-pregnancy-mismatch/", "https://www.healthline.com/health/pregnancy/intermittent-fasting-while-pregnant", "https://bastyr.edu/about/news/5-times-you-should-avoid-intermittent-fasting", "https://www.cnet.com/health/parenting/reasons-intermittent-fasting-is-not-safe-for-pregnant-people/", "https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41652176/", "https://health.clevelandclinic.org/can-you-do-intermittent-fasting-while-breastfeeding", "https://llli.org/fasting/", "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7001685/", "https://diabetesjournals.org/books/book/33/chapter/5202213/A-Case-of-Prolonged-Hypoglycemia-Due-to", "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9182892/", "https://www.sciencedirect.com/study/NCT06887543"]}
